title: Sequential Circuits and Automata

definitions.description: A circuit whose output depends on both the current input and the internal state, which evolves over time.
A level-active bistable circuit: state changes while the clock signal is at the active level.
An edge-active bistable circuit: state changes only on the rising (or falling) edge of the clock signal.
Basic bistable: S=1,R=0 sets Q=1; R=1,S=0 resets Q=0; S=R=0 holds state; S=R=1 is forbidden.
Transparent latch: Q follows D when clock is active, holds otherwise. No forbidden combinations.
Like RS but J=K=1 inverts the stored bit instead of being forbidden.
Toggle latch derived from JK with J=K=T: T=0 holds, T=1 inverts.
A register where data shifts by one position on each clock cycle.
A model of computation with a finite set of states, transitions between states on inputs, and outputs based on state/input.
Implementing the combinational part of a sequential circuit using a ROM: ROM addresses are inputs; ROM data are next-state/output values.
An error-detecting and -correcting code using 4 information bits and 3 parity bits.

formulas.name: Binary counter n=4, D flip-flop equations
formulas.description: d0 = NOT q0; d1 = q1 XOR q0; d2 = q2 XOR (q1 AND q0); d3 = q3 XOR (q2 AND q1 AND q0)

algorithms.name: Sequential circuit design procedure
steps: Determine the states of the circuit
Determine state transitions (next state and output as function of inputs and current state)
Encode states using the necessary number of bits
Write the truth table for state transitions
Implement: flip-flops for state, minimized expressions for combinational part

algorithms.name: Microprogramming approach
algorithms.description: Map the truth table of the next-state function to ROM locations; address = current state + inputs; data = next state + outputs.

examples.name: RS bistable stable-state analysis
examples.description: Full Karnaugh-based analysis of stable states for all (R,S) combinations.

examples.name: 4-bit binary counter
examples.description: 16-state counter with D flip-flop implementations; state table and minimized equations given.

examples.name: ROM-based 4-bit counter
examples.description: Microprogramming implementation showing ROM contents for the 16-state counter.

exercises: Implement and analyze the no-clock RS circuit using NAND gates
Implement RS, D, JK, T latches with NAND gates
Why can shift registers only be built with flip-flops (not latches)?
Homework: prove that C1 representation value is negative when the sign bit is 1
learning_objectives: Distinguish synchronous from asynchronous sequential circuits
Analyze the RS bistable circuit and identify stable states
Understand the difference between latches and flip-flops
Describe and compare RS, D, JK, T bistable circuits
Build registers and counters from bistable circuits
Design a sequential circuit using the finite state machine approach
Apply microprogramming as an alternative circuit implementation technique
Understand error detection and correction (parity, Hamming)
